reasoning: On May 13, 2025, Mali's transitional military government enacted a presidential decree formally dissolving all political parties. This action is part of a deliberate and broader scheme to consolidate power, following national consultations recommending a renewable five-year presidential term for junta leader Assimi Goïta. The timeline for a reversal before July 1, 2025, is exceptionally short (less than 6 weeks). Regimes rarely reverse major power-consolidating decrees so quickly unless forced by existential pressure. While an opposition coalition has protested, these demonstrations took place prior to the formal decree and failed to deter the junta. Instead, authorities responded by arresting opposition figures and suppressing media coverage. Furthermore, Mali's withdrawal from ECOWAS and alignment with fellow junta-led nations (Niger, Burkina Faso) significantly decreases its vulnerability to regional diplomatic leverage. Given the regime's demonstrated willingness to use repression and the lack of overwhelming external or internal forces capable of coercing an immediate reversal, the probability of the decree being overturned by July 2025 is extremely low.
